exercises.title: Which overload runs?
exercises.scenario: Trace through an overloaded method call.
hints: show(10) — 10 is int, calls show(int)
show("Hi") — "Hi" is String, calls show(String)
Java picks based on argument types

exercises.title: Add overload for double
exercises.scenario: An add(int, int) exists. Add a double version.
exercises.instructions: Complete the second add method so it accepts and returns double.
hints: Return type: double
Both parameters: double
Body stays the same: return a + b
Java picks the right add() based on argument types

exercises.title: Three-parameter overload
exercises.scenario: Add a third overload that takes three numbers.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Sum` with TWO overloaded `total` methods:
1. `total(int a, int b)` returns a + b
2. `total(int a, int b, int c)` returns a + b + c

main calls both:
```
total(5, 3): 8
total(5, 3, 2): 10
```
hints: Two methods with the same name (total)
First takes 2 ints, second takes 3 ints
Java picks based on number of arguments
Different parameter COUNT makes them overloads

exercises.title: Fix the illegal overload
exercises.scenario: This code won't compile. Find why.
exercises.instructions:
This code attempts overloading but won't compile. Fix it by changing the SECOND method's signature so it's a valid overload.

The second method should take a double and return a double.

Expected output:
8
4.0

bugs.issue: Two methods can't have identical parameter signatures. Return type alone doesn't differentiate.
bugs.fix: Change parameter types to double, double

hints: Error: 'method calculate(int, int) is already defined'
Return type alone can't differentiate overloads
Change parameters to (double, double)
Now the two methods have different signatures

exercises.title: Overload for String concatenation
exercises.scenario: Build an add that works for ints AND Strings.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Combiner` with TWO `combine` overloads:
1. `combine(int a, int b)` returns sum as int
2. `combine(String a, String b)` returns concatenation as String

main demonstrates both:
```
combine ints: 8
combine strings: Hello World
```

test_cases.expected_stdout:
combine ints: 8
combine strings: Hello World

hints: Both methods named `combine`
Different parameter types: (int, int) vs (String, String)
For Strings, `+` is concatenation; for ints, `+` is addition
Body is the same expression: a + b (Java picks the right operation)

exercises.title: Type promotion in overload
exercises.scenario: When the exact type isn't available, Java promotes.
hints: show(5): 5 is int → exact match for show(int)
show(5L): 5L is long. No show(long) exists. Java promotes long → double. Calls show(double).
show(5.0): 5.0 is double → exact match for show(double)
Promotion path: byte → short → int → long → float → double

exercises.title: Calculate area with multiple shapes
exercises.scenario: Build an area calculator that handles different shape parameter counts.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Area` with three overloaded `area` methods:
1. `area(double radius)` — circle area (pi * r^2)
2. `area(double width, double height)` — rectangle area
3. `area(double base, double height, boolean isTriangle)` — triangle area (0.5 * base * height), third param ignored, exists to differentiate from rectangle

main demonstrates all three:
```
Circle: 78.53981633974483
Rectangle: 50.0
Triangle: 25.0
```
hints: Three methods, all named area, all return double
Circle: 1 parameter (radius)
Rectangle: 2 parameters (width, height)
Triangle: 3 parameters (base, height, isTriangle) — the boolean is just to differentiate from rectangle
Better in real code: separate names like circleArea, rectArea, triangleArea — but this teaches overloading

exercises.title: Print method overloads
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Printer` with FOUR `display` overloads:
1. `display()` — prints '[empty]'
2. `display(int n)` — prints 'int: <n>'
3. `display(double n)` — prints 'double: <n>'
4. `display(String s)` — prints 'string: <s>'

main demonstrates all four:
```
[empty]
int: 42
double: 3.14
string: hello
```
hints: Four overloads of display
Zero-parameter version: just print [empty]
Different parameter types make them legal overloads
Java picks based on argument type at the call site

exercises.title: Fix the parameter-name-only overload
exercises.scenario: Trying to overload by parameter NAME doesn't work.
exercises.instructions:
Fix this code. The intent: two overloads — one with int parameters, one with double. Currently both have int. Fix the second one.

Expected output:
int sum: 7
double sum: 7.5

bugs.issue: Parameter names (x, y vs a, b) don't differentiate methods. Only TYPES matter.
bugs.fix: Change parameter types to double, double

hints: Error message: 'method sum(int, int) is already defined'
Java doesn't care about parameter NAMES
It only looks at TYPES and COUNT
Change parameter types to double, double to make a valid overload

exercises.title: Build a greeting library
exercises.scenario: Real apps have flexible greeting functions — handle different amounts of info.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Greeter` with three `greet` overloads:
1. `greet()` — prints 'Hello, friend!'
2. `greet(String name)` — prints 'Hello, <name>!'
3. `greet(String name, String title)` — prints 'Hello, <title> <name>!'

main calls all three:
```
Hello, friend!
Hello, Aisha!
Hello, Dr. Sharma!
```

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Hello, friend!
Hello, Aisha!
Hello, Dr. Sharma!

hints: Three overloads of greet
Different parameter counts: 0, 1, 2
Note: greet("Sharma", "Dr.") passes name FIRST, title SECOND
Output format: 'Hello, <title> <name>!'

exercises.title: Varargs introduction
exercises.scenario: Use Java's varargs feature to accept any number of arguments.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Sum` with ONE method using VARARGS:
- `sumAll(int... numbers)` — returns sum of all numbers passed

main calls it with different numbers of arguments:
```
sumAll(): 0
sumAll(5): 5
sumAll(1, 2, 3): 6
sumAll(10, 20, 30, 40, 50): 150
```
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Sum class below
// Use varargs: int... numbers
hints: Varargs syntax: int... numbers (three dots)
Inside the method, `numbers` behaves like an array
Use enhanced for loop: for (int n : numbers)
Accumulator: total starts at 0, add each n
Can call with 0, 1, or many arguments — Java handles it

exercises.title: Overload + promotion trace
exercises.scenario: Predict tricky overload resolution with type promotion.
exercises.expected_solution:
int version
double version
double version
double version

test_cases.expected:
int version
double version
double version
double version

hints: process(10): 10 is int → exact match for process(int)
process(10L): 10L is long. No process(long). Java promotes to double. Calls process(double).
process(10.0f): 10.0f is float. No process(float). Java promotes to double. Calls process(double).
process(10.0): 10.0 is double → exact match for process(double)
Result: int, double, double, double

exercises.title: Build overloaded logger
exercises.scenario: Build a Logger class like real logging libraries.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Logger` with FOUR overloaded `log` methods:

1. `log(String message)` — prints '[INFO] <message>'
2. `log(String level, String message)` — prints '[<level>] <message>'
3. `log(String message, int errorCode)` — prints '[ERROR <code>] <message>'
4. `log(String level, String message, int errorCode)` — prints '[<level> <code>] <message>'

main demonstrates all four:
```
[INFO] Server started
[WARN] Low memory
[ERROR 404] Page not found
[FATAL 500] Database crash
```

test_cases.expected_stdout:
[INFO] Server started
[WARN] Low memory
[ERROR 404] Page not found
[FATAL 500] Database crash

hints: Four overloads of log
Different parameter combinations: (String), (String, String), (String, int), (String, String, int)
Notice: overloads 2 and 3 both have 2 params but different TYPES — that makes them distinct
This is exactly how real loggers (Log4j, SLF4J) work

exercises.title: Format method overloads
exercises.scenario: Build a formatting utility with multiple overloads for currency display.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Currency` with THREE `format` overloads:

1. `format(double amount)` — returns String like '₹<amount>'
2. `format(double amount, String symbol)` — returns String like '<symbol><amount>'
3. `format(double amount, String symbol, boolean showDecimal)` — if showDecimal=false, returns just integer part; if true, full amount

main demonstrates all three:
```
₹1500.0
$1500.0
€1500
```
hints: All three methods return String
First overload: default currency is ₹
Second overload: caller chooses symbol
Third overload: also chooses whether to show decimals
For no decimals: cast to (int) to drop the fractional part
1500.0 cast to int → 1500

exercises.title: Build complete shape area calculator with overloads
exercises.scenario: Build a comprehensive shape area calculator using both overloading and varargs.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `ShapeCalculator` with these methods:

1. `area(double side)` — square area (side * side)
2. `area(double width, double height)` — rectangle area
3. `area(double base, double height, boolean isTriangle)` — triangle area (0.5 * base * height)
4. `totalArea(double... areas)` — varargs method that sums all given areas

In main():
- Calculate area of: square (side 5), rectangle (4x6), triangle (base 3, height 8)
- Use totalArea() to sum all three

Expected output:
Square area: 25.0
Rectangle area: 24.0
Triangle area: 12.0
Total area: 61.0

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Square area: 25.0
Rectangle area: 24.0
Triangle area: 12.0
Total area: 61.0

hints: Three overloads of area: 1, 2, and 3 parameters
Square: side * side
Rectangle: width * height
Triangle: 0.5 * base * height (boolean parameter just differentiates from rectangle)
totalArea uses varargs: double... areas
Inside totalArea: enhanced for loop, accumulator pattern
